reading_plan.reading_goal: Reconstruct the research evolution trajectory of WISDOM-II, focusing on the problem of malaria drug resistance and the need for multi-target screening, the constraints of computational cost and data management, the hypotheses about target selection and docking parameter optimization, the design of the grid-enabled virtual screening pipeline, the computational experiments (re-docking, large-scale screening, result analysis), observations from docking scores and interaction patterns, failures or partial results (e.g., suboptimal binding modes in WISDOM-I, challenges in result storage), revisions in analysis strategy (e.g., incorporating interaction-based filtering), validations (re-docking, comparison with known inhibitors, diversity analysis), and limitations (e.g., lack of experimental validation, reliance on docking scores).

gist_memory.paper_problem_memory: Malaria drug resistance necessitates discovery of new drugs targeting multiple proteins. Virtual high-throughput screening (vHTS) is cost-effective but computationally intensive. WISDOM-I screened plasmepsins on grids, identifying novel scaffolds. WISDOM-II extends to multiple targets (PfGST, PfDHFR, PvDHFR) to counteract resistance, leveraging grid computing for large-scale docking of 4.3M compounds from ZINC. Key challenges include computational cost, data management, and parameter optimization for docking accuracy.

section_gists.heading: Abstract
section_gists.gist: Summarizes WISDOM-II: grid-enabled vHTS against multiple malaria targets (DHFR, GST) using FlexX. Builds on WISDOM-I success. Results stored in MySQL, refined with MD and MM-PBSA/GBSA rescoring. In vitro testing underway.
process_signals: WISDOM-I identified novel plasmepsin inhibitors confirmed in vitro.
WISDOM-II focuses on DHFR and GST to address resistance.
Sustained infrastructure developed for large-scale docking and result analysis.

section_gists.heading: Background
section_gists.gist: Hit discovery for neglected diseases via in silico screening on grids is cost-effective. WISDOM-II aims to find hits against multiple targets to combat multi-drug resistance. Fits between target portfolio and preclinical research.
process_signals: Need for high-risk early discovery phase.
First large-scale in silico drug finding against malaria.
Enables affordable in vitro tests for less developed countries.

section_gists.heading: WISDOM, a virtual docking service on grids
section_gists.gist: vHTS is complementary to HTS, but computationally intensive. Grids solve this. WISDOM-I docked 500K compounds against plasmepsins, found novel scaffolds confirmed in vitro. WISDOM-II aims to identify inhibitors and develop a fault-tolerant production environment.
process_signals: WISDOM-I: 42M dockings, 80 CPU years, identified guanidino scaffold.
WISDOM-II goals: identify inhibitors, develop fault-tolerant environment.
Targets initially malaria, expandable.
failures_or_limits: WISDOM-I focused on single target family (plasmepsins).

section_gists.heading: The grid enabled virtual screening process
section_gists.gist: WISDOM-II extends to multiple targets from P. falciparum and P. vivax to interfere with resistance. Develops new procedures for storage, post-processing, and analysis.
process_signals: Shift from single target family to multiple targets due to resistance complexity.
Inclusion of P. vivax due to resurgence.
Development of new storage and analysis strategies.

section_gists.heading: Target structures
section_gists.gist: Introduces the biological targets used in the study.

section_gists.heading: Glutathione-S-transferase
section_gists.gist: PfGST is a detoxification enzyme; its inhibition impairs antioxidant defense. In chloroquine-resistant parasites, GST activity correlates with drug pressure. Structure: dimer with G and H sites; H site is solvent-accessible due to short μ-loop.
process_signals: Rationale: GST inhibition reduces detoxification and antioxidant capacity.
Target selection based on role in drug resistance.

section_gists.heading: Plasmodium vivax and P. falciparum DHFR
section_gists.gist: DHFR is target of antifolates; resistance arises from point mutations. P. vivax DHFR resistance involves structural change at Asn-108. Mutations in PfDHFR at positions 16,51,59,108,164 cause cross-resistance. Crystal structures available.
process_signals: Target selection based on known resistance mechanisms.
Use of wild-type and mutant structures to find inhibitors overcoming resistance.

section_gists.heading: Virtual docking procedure
section_gists.gist: Overview of the virtual docking steps.

section_gists.heading: Target preparation
section_gists.gist: Target structures from PDB; active site defined as 8-10 Å around co-crystallized ligand.
methods: Active site definition: 8-10 Å around co-crystallized ligand

section_gists.heading: Glutathione-S-transferase (GST)
section_gists.gist: Used PDB 1Q4J, removed crystal waters, defined active site 8 Å around GTX. Re-docking performed for parameter optimization.
process_signals: Re-docking used to optimize target and software parameters.

section_gists.heading: Plasmodium vivax DHFR (PvDHFR) and Plasmodium falciparum DHFR (PfDHFR)
section_gists.gist: Used crystal structures of wild-type and mutant DHFRs. Missing residues modeled with Modeller. Energy minimization and MD refinement with AMBER9/ff03. Conserved water molecules retained.
process_signals: Modeling missing residues to complete structures.
Refinement with minimization and MD to optimize hydrogen positions.
methods: Energy minimization (500 steps H-only, 5000 steps all-atom)
50 ps MD at 300K on hydrogens with heavy atom restraints

section_gists.heading: Compound database
section_gists.gist: ZINC database of 4.3 million compounds used, available in multiple formats.
process_signals: Choice of ZINC for open access and ready-to-dock formats.

section_gists.heading: FlexX: Docking software
section_gists.gist: FlexX used for docking. Parameters 'Place particles' and 'Maximum overlap volume' varied per target. Re-docking validates protocol by comparing RMSD and interactions to co-crystallized pose.
process_signals: No generic parameter set; optimization via re-docking.
Re-docking serves as positive control before large-scale screening.
methods: Parameter variation: Place particles, Maximum overlap volume
Re-docking validation: RMSD, visual inspection, interaction comparison

section_gists.heading: Re-docking
section_gists.gist: Re-docking results: PfGST had RMSD 3.68 Å but convincing binding mode. PvDHFR re-docking failed (high RMSD, poor binding). PfDHFR re-docking succeeded after minimization and increasing overlap volume (parameter 8, 100 Å³) gave RMSD <2 Å and correct interactions. Known inhibitors also docked.
process_signals: PfGST: high RMSD but acceptable interactions.
PvDHFR: re-docking failed due to clashes.
PfDHFR: success after structure minimization and parameter tuning (overlap volume).
Parameter 8 (overlap 100 Å³) best for PfDHFR.
failures_or_limits: PvDHFR re-docking failed (high RMSD, poor binding mode).
PfGST RMSD >2 Å, though binding mode acceptable.

global_hypotheses_about_storyline: Multi-target screening is necessary to overcome drug resistance in malaria.
Grid computing enables cost-effective large-scale vHTS.
Target-specific parameter optimization (e.g., overlap volume) is critical for docking accuracy.
Re-docking and interaction analysis validate docking protocols before large-scale screening.
Inclusion of mutant DHFR structures may identify inhibitors effective against resistant strains.
uncertainties_to_resolve: Why PvDHFR re-docking failed and whether alternative protocols could improve it.
How the high RMSD for PfGST affects the reliability of subsequent screening hits.
Whether the interaction-based filtering compensates for suboptimal RMSD in hit selection.
Outcome of in vitro testing for selected hits.
Scalability and fault-tolerance of the grid infrastructure for other targets.

events.summary: Malaria drug resistance necessitates discovery of new drugs targeting multiple proteins.
WISDOM-II extends virtual screening to multiple targets from P. falciparum and P. vivax to interfere with resistance.
Grid computing is used to overcome the computational intensity of large-scale virtual screening.
WISDOM-I performed 42 million dockings against plasmepsins, identifying novel scaffolds confirmed in vitro.
Inhibition of GST will impair detoxification and reduce antioxidant capacity of the parasite.
Antifolate resistance in DHFR arises from point mutations, necessitating screening against mutant forms.
New procedures for storage, post-processing, and analysis of docking results are being developed.

chunk_events.notes: Chunk covers abstract, background, WISDOM service description, grid-enabled process overview, and target structure introductions. Contains explicit problem statements, design decisions, and references to prior experimental results.

events.summary: Target preparation: active site defined as 8-10 Å around co-crystallized ligands.
GST target preparation: used PDB 1Q4J, removed crystal waters, defined active site 8 Å around GTX, performed re-docking for parameter optimization.
DHFR target preparation: modeled missing residues with Modeller, refined with AMBER9 minimization and MD, retained two conserved waters.
Compound database: used ZINC database of 4.3 million compounds for screening.
Docking software: FlexX used with varied parameters 'Place particles' and 'Maximum overlap volume' optimized per target via re-docking.

Re-docking validation: PfGST re-docking gave RMSD 3.68 Å but convincing binding mode; PvDHFR re-docking failed with high RMSD and poor binding; PfDHFR re-docking succeeded after minimization and parameter tuning (parameter 8, overlap 100 Å³) with RMSD <2 Å.
PvDHFR re-docking failed due to clashes between protein and ligand atom surfaces.
Re-docking serves as positive control before large-scale screening to validate docking protocol.

chunk_events.notes: Chunk covers target preparation, compound database, docking software setup, and re-docking validation experiments, which are core to the virtual screening process.

events.summary: Re-docking against minimized PfDHFR structures reproduced binding mode with RMSD <2 Å.
Parameter 8 (overlap volume 100 Å³) gave best results for PfDHFR docking.
Virtual screening performed on 4.3 million compounds against multiple targets using EGEE grid.
Improved WISDOM production environment with automatic job resubmission enabled smooth deployment.

chunk_events.notes: Chunk covers re-docking validation, grid deployment, database design, and initial docking result analysis for PfGST.

events.summary: Diversity analysis of top scoring compounds for PfGST and PfDHFR using MOE software and Tanimoto coefficient to assess compound diversity.
Diversity analysis for PfDHFR top 15,000 compounds shows diverse dataset with mean TC 0.42.
Modeling of final hits against PfGST: ligand plots generated and interactions studied, showing comparable binding poses to co-crystallized ligand GTX.
Hydrogen bonding interactions of best compounds with PfGST are listed in Table 6, showing distances and types.
Handling large data output is a major bottleneck; addressed by parsing results into MySQL database storing docking score and atom-to-atom interactions.
Future work aims to extend virtual screening pipeline with additional analysis methods and tighter integration of in silico prediction and experimental validation.
Data handling and analysis methods need significant improvement; future plans include learning from series of docking experiments and adding predictive toxicology and ADME models.
Diversity analysis revealed top scoring compounds are diverse and sensible for further analysis, validating the selection for hit identification.

canonical_events.summary: Malaria drug resistance necessitates discovery of new drugs targeting multiple proteins.
WISDOM-II extends virtual screening to multiple targets from P. falciparum and P. vivax to interfere with resistance.
Grid computing is used to overcome the computational intensity of large-scale virtual screening.
WISDOM-I performed 42 million dockings against plasmepsins, identifying novel scaffolds confirmed in vitro.
Inhibition of GST will impair detoxification and reduce antioxidant capacity of the parasite.
Antifolate resistance in DHFR arises from point mutations, necessitating screening against mutant forms.
Target preparation: active site defined as 8-10 Å around co-crystallized ligands.
GST target preparation: used PDB 1Q4J, removed crystal waters, defined active site 8 Å around GTX, performed re-docking for parameter optimization.
DHFR target preparation: modeled missing residues with Modeller, refined with AMBER9 minimization and MD, retained two conserved waters.
Compound database: used ZINC database of 4.3 million compounds for screening.
Docking software: FlexX used with varied parameters 'Place particles' and 'Maximum overlap volume' optimized per target via re-docking.

Re-docking validation: PfGST re-docking gave RMSD 3.68 Å but convincing binding mode; PvDHFR re-docking failed with high RMSD and poor binding; PfDHFR re-docking succeeded after minimization and parameter tuning (parameter 8, overlap 100 Å³) with RMSD <2 Å.
PvDHFR re-docking failed due to clashes between protein and ligand atom surfaces.
Virtual screening performed on 4.3 million compounds against multiple targets using EGEE grid.
Improved WISDOM production environment with automatic job resubmission enabled smooth deployment.
Diversity analysis of top scoring compounds for PfGST and PfDHFR using MOE software and Tanimoto coefficient to assess compound diversity.
Modeling of final hits against PfGST: ligand plots generated and interactions studied, showing comparable binding poses to co-crystallized ligand GTX.
Handling large data output is a major bottleneck; addressed by parsing results into MySQL database storing docking score and atom-to-atom interactions.

source.title: WISDOM-II: Screening against multiple targets implicated in malaria using computational grid infrastructures

initial_request.research_problem: Malaria drug resistance necessitates discovery of new drugs targeting multiple proteins. Virtual high-throughput screening (vHTS) is cost-effective but computationally intensive. WISDOM-II extends to multiple targets (PfGST, PfDHFR, PvDHFR) to counteract resistance, leveraging grid computing for large-scale docking of 4.3M compounds from ZINC.
initial_request.target_object: Multiple malaria targets: PfGST, PfDHFR (wild-type and quadruple mutant), PvDHFR (wild-type and double mutant)
initial_request.known_context: WISDOM-I successfully screened 42 million compounds against plasmepsins, identifying novel scaffolds confirmed in vitro. Drug resistance in DHFR arises from point mutations. GST inhibition impairs detoxification and antioxidant capacity.
constraints: Computational intensity of large-scale virtual screening
Need for validated target structures and docking parameters
quantifiable_goals: Identify novel inhibitors against multiple malaria targets
Achieve successful re-docking (RMSD < 2 Å for PfDHFR)

evolution_trajectory.state_before: Malaria drug resistance is a major problem; single-target drugs are insufficient.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Need to target multiple proteins and pathways to counteract resistance.
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: Virtual screening against multiple validated targets from P. falciparum and P. vivax will identify novel inhibitors and interfere with resistance.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Design WISDOM-II to screen against PfGST, PfDHFR, and PvDHFR.
evolution_trajectory.observation: Targets selected: PfGST (detoxification), PfDHFR (folate pathway, mutations cause resistance), PvDHFR (also important for vivax malaria).
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Need to prepare target structures and optimize docking parameters.

evidence.quote_or_span: due to complexity of the life cycle and drug resistance more targets and more metabolic pathways have to be targeted to counteract the disease
in the current project: WISDOM-II, different validated targets involved in diverse metabolic activities of the parasite were selected
proteins not only from Plasmodium falciparum, but also from the Plasmodium vivax were embattled

evolution_trajectory.state_before: Targets selected; need computational resources for large-scale docking.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Virtual screening of millions of compounds is computationally intensive.
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: Grid computing can provide the necessary computational power.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Use EGEE grid infrastructure and other grids for WISDOM-II.
evolution_trajectory.observation: Grid infrastructures successfully used in WISDOM-I for large-scale dockings.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Proceed to target preparation and docking parameter optimization.

evidence.quote_or_span: Computational grid infrastructures are the best attempt to solving this problem thus far.
The computing resources from EGEE grid infrastructure were utilized extensively
three scaffolds were identified, one of them is guanidino scaffold, which is likely to be novel

evolution_trajectory.state_before: Grid infrastructure ready; need to prepare target structures for docking.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Accurate active site definition and protein structure preparation required.
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: Defining active site around co-crystallized ligands and refining structures will enable reliable docking.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Prepare PfGST, PfDHFR, and PvDHFR structures: define active site, model missing residues, minimize, and retain conserved waters.

parameters.minimization: 500 steps H-only, 5000 steps all-atom, 50 ps MD at 300K, 5000 steps final minimization

evolution_trajectory.observation: Structures prepared: PfGST (1Q4J, active site 8 Å around GTX), PfDHFR (missing residues modeled, two conserved waters kept), PvDHFR (similarly prepared).
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Need to select compound database and docking software.

evidence.quote_or_span: active site is defined as 8.0 - 10 around the co-crystallized ligands.
The active site is defined as 8 around the co-crystallized ligand: GTX
the position of missing residues from Asp87 to Asn90 was modelled with Modeller software
All water molecules in the crystal structure were removed except for two conserved waters

evolution_trajectory.state_before: Targets prepared; need compound library and docking software.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Selection of appropriate compound database and docking parameters.
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: ZINC database of 4.3 million compounds is suitable; FlexX with optimized parameters will yield accurate docking poses.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Use ZINC database and FlexX; optimize parameters per target via re-docking.
evolution_trajectory.observation: ZINC database downloaded; FlexX selected; parameter optimization initiated.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Validate docking parameters through re-docking experiments.

evidence.quote_or_span: The ZINC database is a collection of 4.3 million chemical compounds
A total of 4.3 million compounds were downloaded from the ZINC database and screened against targets

evolution_trajectory.state_before: Docking parameters need validation.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Whether FlexX parameters can reproduce known binding modes.
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: Re-docking co-crystallized ligands will validate parameter sets.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Perform re-docking for PfGST, PfDHFR, and PvDHFR with various parameter sets.
evolution_trajectory.observation: PfGST: convincing binding mode but RMSD not optimal. PfDHFR: after minimization, parameter 8 (overlap 100 Å³) gave RMSD <2 Å. PvDHFR: re-docking failed due to clashes.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Proceed with validated parameters for PfGST and PfDHFR; PvDHFR excluded from further screening.

evidence.quote_or_span: Parameter 8 (maximum allowed overlap volume between protein and ligand surface: 100 Å³) gave the best results
This is due to clashes between the protein and ligand atom surfaces.

Re-docking against minimized structures with the same parameter sets gave best results. All the parameter sets reproduced the actual binding mode of the ligand, further made interactions to key amino acids and RMS deviation were less than 2 Å.
Parameter 8 (maximum allowed overlap volume between protein and ligand surface: 100 Å³) gave the best results in terms of docking score, docking conformation and interactions to key amino acids.

evolution_trajectory.state_before: Validated parameters for PfGST and PfDHFR; PvDHFR excluded.

evidence.quote_or_span: virtual screening was performed on 4.3 million compounds against the targets specified in Table 1.

The significant achievement on the grid side is the improved WISDOM production environment, which enabled smooth and successful deployment of docking jobs on the grid. The major difference between our previous deployments (WISDOM-I and the data challenge on avian flu) is the automatic resubmission of the docking jobs on the grid.

evolution_trajectory.decision: Perform diversity analysis on top 5,000 PfGST and 15,000 PfDHFR compounds using MOE software.
evolution_trajectory.observation: PfGST: 3,394 clusters identified. Compounds are diverse.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Model interactions of top PfGST hits.

evidence.quote_or_span: diversity analysis against the PfGST best 5,000 compounds and PfDHFR best 15,000 compounds by docking score was performed by using MOE software
